I thought I would start out by telling you about one of my greatest failures, because you are all going to have a failure at some point in your career. How you choose to act after that happens is going to determine everything. I had been working as an in-house writer for Schafer's, and many of you already heard my passionate pitch for starting out as an in-house writer. I had just broken out and left Schafer's. I had been reading everything the Motley Fool wrote. I loved the Motley Fool. I wanted to work for them. I said to Denise, I was still working as a freelancer with Schafer's, and I was looking for other writers at Bootcamp, and I said, I really worship those guys at the Fool. They're so cool. I love their copy. It's so fun. Would you put my Bootcamp table next to theirs in the like financial circle? She's like, sure honey, I'll do that. I mean, you know Denise, right? She'll do anything. I'm like here and I keep looking over and there's like Paul Elliott, and I'm like, they're right next to me. Every now and then there'd be a break in the flow of people at Job Fear coming through, and I just like, how are things at the Fool? How's it going? Just struck up a conversation. The next time I meet him was at Clayton's Big Conference for the Total Package, the lunch conference, and the Motley Fool guys are there again. I'm like, hey, how's it going? We're chatting and we're talking, and Bern White is with him this time. It was when Paul and Bern were the entire copywriting team at the Motley Fool. Bern says, we'll show us some samples. So I send him over some samples. He likes them, long story short. He says, well, why don't you give a shot writing for one of our products? I said, I would love to do this. He gives me a product to write for, and it's a stock tease promotion, which they're so hard for me to do. One, because I tell everybody everything, so the idea of holding a whole bunch of details back doesn't work very well. Okay. You have to reveal just enough but not give away the stock symbol. So I slave over this thing for eight weeks, and I am studying controls and I'm trying to write it. What the technology was, it was a screening technology for TSA at the airports. This was like it could find anything on a human being. I'm sure it could probably tell you if you had liver cancer as you went through. So they were going to put this in all the airports. This was after 9-11 when security was a huge deal. We're all trying to catch up. So I write this promotion. I'm talking about how this is going to save the world. This is the next greatest thing. We're going to make a fortune. They queue it up to go out. It's an email. The Motley Fool list is massive. You probably know more than I do. How many numbers on it? It goes out and this is what happens when you live in the Internet world. It's not like you wait six days to find out. You're going to find out in six minutes. So I'm like, waiting, I'm waiting, I'm waiting. I hear anything all day and finally I call Burn and I'm like, do you know what's going on? He said, well, we don't have a single sale. He says, I think clearly we've got some technology. Glitch here and my guys are on it. We're trying to figure out. We're testing all the links. We're going to put in a couple test orders. We're going to send it out again tomorrow morning. So I'm like, oh man, you know, I'm like, okay, okay, calm down, calm down. We'll go out tomorrow morning. They send it out the next morning. They get two orders, the two test orders that they put in. Everything is working. It did not sell a single, not one newsletter. And in our postmortem, what we decided is that people were offended. They thought I was telling them how to make money on the people who died in 9-11. I thought I was telling them this will save our country. This is the best technology. This will never happen again. We could write. That was not what they heard. They heard you want me to make money off of 9-11. Okay, so already I'm feeling like, right? In that moment, I had a couple choices. I could have said clearly I can only write in-house fast dirty options copy for Schaffers. Clearly I'm not made to be a six-figure copywriter. Clearly I'm not made to do this on my own as a freelancer. Clearly I have no idea what I'm doing. The Motley Fool will never talk to me again. You know that voice when it gets going? It talks even faster than I'm talking now. It's like this little guy with a hammer going bam, bam, bam, bam, bam, bam, bam. But I didn't do that. What I said to them is I feel terrible about this. I am so sorry. And Bern said, it's not your fault. We all read this. We thought this was great copy. We chiefted it with you. And I said, I know. But I feel really bad about this and I want to give you another promotion for free. Just anything you want me to write on, I'll give you another promotion for free. Now I have done this at multiple times in my career. So many times in fact that David has to threaten to shoot me when I continue to do it at this point. But in the beginning it was a good strategy. So they said, oh, you don't have to do that. I said, no, I'm serious. Give me something else. Now they're not stupid. The next one they gave me was dead on arrival. It was a product that no one had been. I mean, I guess they figured the worst I could do was just leave it dead. Right? And it was this product called Greenlight. And it was a whole bunch of little tips. So it'd be like how to get a pair of Jimmy shoes for $5 at the secret website that no one knows about. The girls know what I'm talking about. Or it was like how to get one of those high end, temperopedic mattresses for half price, whatever. Okay. So I'm looking at this and I had just started being an apprentice with Paris. And I'm thinking, wow, this is like fascination kind of stuff. And he's teaching me how to do this boardroom style. And Motley Fool's never done that because they do all these stock picks because most of their promotions are about their stock services. And maybe the reason they can't sell this thing is that they're trying to sell it like their other services. But this is really like this little snippets of helpful financial information. I thought, well, why don't I do what Paris is teaching me? One I'll learn because I'll have to practice what he's been teaching me. So I go, okay, so I'll just write a bunch of bullets and then I'll write many articles on some of the things that I know people would want like the Jimmy shoe thing and the bed thing. And so I put it all together and I take David's control. This is another tip for the Weil and sister package. I told you about yesterday with the bug spray and the women in the shorts. I take his control, which has been put online by boardroom and I reverse engineered. I'm like, what did you do? Okay, he had six bullets. Okay, I'm gonna have six bullets. Then what did you do? Oh, then he had a mini. Then he had a mini. Then what did you do? I was like, whatever he did, I just mapped it all the way across, but I put in my information, turned it into them and they're like, wow, this is really different. And I said, yeah, you know, I think we need to, nothing's worked. Let's try something. Okay, so they send it out. It literally resurrected the dead product. And Paris was at the financial publishers news round table. What are the, anyway, financial gurus that are around and whatever. So Paris goes to that and the guy who's the head of the Motley Fool comes up to him and says, you know that copy cub of hers? My God, she brought that thing back from the dead. We've been mailing that promotion nonstop, you know, by email. So when, so I've gone and now here it is. God, I don't know. Now it's maybe six, seven years later and they have this huge copy team in house like Stance Berry and, and burn white runs it. He's a great guy and they have like 12 product launches that they have to get out in 2012 and burns busier than a one arm paper hanger and he can't get through all the work and one of their products is Motley Fool options. Now they have a whole bunch of high end products, but one of their products is Motley Fool options. And burn remembers that I know how to do options that I used to do them nonstop every day at Schafer's. And here's the other thing. This product is a very conservative option trading product and the strategies are more complicated than nuclear fusion, right? And I'm going to try to get people to sign on for this, but you have to kind of set them up and tell them that ahead of time or they get this like shock once they get in. And so you can't just say, come trade options, they're fun. And then they get in and find out there's 15 steps and it's, but it's a strategy that wins consistently all the time because of how he sets them up. So he knows I understand the complicated strategies that they're using. So even though they do not use in house, they do not use freelancers anymore. They have this massive in house team like Stansbury, they're all employees. They can't produce all the copy they need that year. Burn remembers me, remembers everything I've done for them in the past, even though it's these sort of little things that haven't, you know, it's been years, right? And says to them, okay, there's one way to solve this. You could bring in Marcella and have her do the options launch. And my guys and I can do everything else because she knows options probably better than any of us do. So by then I have copy harvest. I have David, chiefing all my stuff. I've got my partner Lois who worked with me at Schaffers for years. So we ran as if we were an in house team, an entire options campaign from start to finish with, I swear to God, it felt like 486 parts, but it was probably what, I don't know, 50 pieces or something with constant communication with their, because they're not used to using freelancers. They use in house people. So I had to turn myself into their in house people flying back and forth, working with them on it. We came in when the campaign was done, we had made 3.4 million in six weeks and we were more than 30% above what they had done the last time. So the moral of the story is when you have that moment, and I really hope none of you actually have your first promotion sells zero, except for the two test orders the guy in IT put in, but when you face that moment, I can't think of anything worse, right? Maybe negative something. I don't know. People showed up and demanded something back. So when you have that happen, you are going to have a choice. And what you do at that moment is going to determine how you go forward. So remember that you are not your failures, okay? You can pick yourself back up. I love Belinda Brewster. She says, I got to pick myself back up. I got to put my big girl panties on. I got to go back out there, right? So that's what you need to do. For me, it's often saying, okay, let me do something else for you. And I'm not saying that's always how you... There will be something. There will be something that comes to you that you need to do to move forward. Whether you say to yourself, okay, I didn't know enough to do this. What do I need to do? I'm going to go back and I get more knowledge. I'm going to go to Clayton's seminar. I'm going to read a book. You will know in that moment what it is that you need to do. And remember that that failure is teaching you. It's like getting it out of the way. It's like checking the box. Okay, I had my first big failure. Now I'm ready. Now I'm going to move on. That failure does not mean you are not meant to be a copywriter. It means you are right where you are supposed to be. You are experiencing what you are supposed to be right now in that moment. And that your next success is just right around the corner. But you got to walk through it. You got to walk through it. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
